# Reconstructing tropical monthly sea surface temperature variability by assimilating coral proxy datasets

[*npj Climate and Atmospheric Science*](/npjclimatsci)
**volume 7**, Article number: 261 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1748 Accesses

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Coral reconstruction often serves as a major proxy of high-resolution sea surface temperature (SST) variability beyond the instrumental era. However, coral reconstructions are sparse and are usually studied for interannual variability, with few studies on the monthly features. In this study, we reconstruct the monthly SST spatial field by applying the paleoclimate data assimilation method to the coral records of the latest CoralHydro2k data set for the instrument period of 1880–2000. A comparison with observed SST variability shows that our assimilated tropical SST variability performs reasonably well for the seasonal cycle and monthly ENSO characteristics, notably the phase-locking and onset timing, and more realistic spatial fields relative to the model simulations. This study suggests the feasibility of applying paleoclimate data assimilation to reconstruct the monthly SST in the historical period.

## Introduction

El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is the major mode of interannual climate variability that exerts significant impacts on global climate, ecosystems, and socio-economic developments[1](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR1 "Timmermann, A. et al. El Niño–Southern Oscillation complexity. Nature 559, 535–545 (2018)."),[2](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR2 "Wang, C. Z. & Fiedler, P. C. ENSO variability and the eastern tropical pacific: A review. Prog. Oceanogr. 69, 239–266 (2006)."). Monthly sea surface temperature (SST) data are crucial to better understand the detailed features of ENSO occurrence and development, including the relationship between onset timing and duration of ENSO events[3](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR3 "Wu, X., Okumura, Y. M. & DiNezio, P. N. What Controls the Duration of El Niño and La Niña Events? J. Clim. 32, 5941–5965 (2019)."), the diversity in the persistence of multi-year El Niño events[4](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR4 "Sanchez, S. C. & Karnauskas, K. B. Diversity in the persistence of El Niño events over the last millennium. Geophys. Res. Lett. 48, e2021GL093698 (2021)."), and the responses of super El Niño events to SSTA in Indian and Atlantic Oceans[5](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR5 "Wang, J.-Z. & Wang, C. Joint boost to super El Niño from the Indian and Atlantic Oceans. J. Clim. 34, 4937–4954 (2021)."). In particular, monthly data is necessary for assessing the phase-locking of ENSO evolution, which is key feature of ENSO and is crucial for ENSO forecasting (e.g[6](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR6 "Jin, E. K. & Kinter, J. L. Characteristics of tropical Pacific SST predictability in coupled GCM forecasts using the NCEP CFS. Clim. Dyn. 32, 675–691 (2009)."),[7](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR7 "Liu, Z., Jin, Y. & Rong, X. A theory for seasonal predictability barrier: threshold, timing and intensity. J. Clim. 32, 423–443 (2019).")).

Paleoclimate data expands the period of instrumental observations (1854–2024 CE), and can provide valuable perspectives on historical ENSO variability. Many recent studies have examined ENSO characteristics in the historical period beyond the instrumental era, especially on the long-term modulations and different responses to natural and anthropogenic forcings[4](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR4 "Sanchez, S. C. & Karnauskas, K. B. Diversity in the persistence of El Niño events over the last millennium. Geophys. Res. Lett. 48, e2021GL093698 (2021)."),[8](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR8 "Li, J. et al. Interdecadal modulation of El Niño amplitude during the past millennium. Nat. Clim. Change 1, 114–118 (2011)."),[9](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR9 "Li, J. et al. El Niño modulations over the past seven centuries. Nat. Clim. Change 3, 822–826 (2013)."). Among different types of paleoclimate proxy records, corals have a large annual growth rate with clear seasonal growth bands, and they also have long growth histories. Coral growth is largely controlled by temperature, light, and carbonate saturation state[10](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR10 "Kleypas, J. A., Mcmanus, J. W. & Menez, L. A. B. Environmental Limits to Coral Reef Development: Where Do We Draw the Line? Am. Zool. 39, 146–159 (1999)."). Average water temperatures are a major environmental control on coral extension and calcification rates, with warmer SST corresponding to stronger extension and calcification rate signal[11](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR11 "Lough, J. Coral calcification from skeletal records revisited. Mar. Ecol. Prog. Ser. 373, 257–264 (2008)."). As a result, corals have become excellent archives in recording high-resolution (i.e., monthly to annual) environmental history of tropical oceans[12](#ref-CR12 "Yu, K. Coral reefs in the South China Sea: Their response to and records on past environmental changes. Sci. China Earth Sci. 55, 1217–1229 (2012)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Cobb, K. M., Charles, C. D., Cheng, H. & Edwards, R. L. El Niño/Southern Oscillation and tropical Pacific climate during the last millennium. Nature 424, 271–276 (2003)."),[14](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR14 "Gagan, M. K. et al. New views of tropical paleoclimates from corals. Quat. Sci. Rev. 19, 45–64 (2000)."). The \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) of coral skeleton has been used as an indicator of the variability of SST and seawater oxygen isotope composition (\({\delta }^{18}{{\rm{O}}}\_{{\rm{sw}}}\))[15](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR15 "Epstein, S., Buchsbaum, R., Lowenstam, H. A. & Urey, H. C. Revised carbonate-water isotopic temperature scale. Geol. Soc. Am. Bull. 64, 1315–1326 (1953)."),[16](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR16 "Weber, J. N. & Woodhead, P. M. J. Temperature dependence of oxygen-18 concentration in reef coral carbonates. J. Geophys. 77, 463–473 (1972)."). Previous studies have shown that coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstructions in the central Pacific are highly correlated with ENSO activity[17](#ref-CR17 "Cobb, K. M., Charles, C. D. & Hunter, D. E. A central tropical Pacific coral demonstrates Pacific, Indian, and Atlantic decadal climate connections. Geophys. Res. Lett. 28, 2209–2212 (2001)."),[18](#ref-CR18 "McGregor, H. V. et al. A weak El Niño/Southern Oscillation with delayed seasonal growth around 4,300 years ago. Nat. Geosci. 6, 949–953 (2013)."),[19](#ref-CR19 "McGregor, H. V., Fischer, M. J., Gagan, M. K., Fink, D. & Woodroffe, C. D. Environmental control of the oxygen isotope composition of Porites coral microatolls. Geochim. Cosmochim. Ac. 75, 3930–3944 (2011)."),[20](#ref-CR20 "Evans, M. N., Fairbanks, R. G. & Rubenstone, J. L. A proxy index of ENSO teleconnections. Nature 394, 732–733 (1998)."),[21](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR21 "Nurhati, I. S., Cobb, K. M., Charles, C. D. & Dunbar, R. B. Late 20th century warming and freshening in the central tropical Pacific. Geophys. Res. Lett. 36, L21606 (2009)."). When El Niño occurs, the increased SST and decreased salinity that is caused by intensified precipitation jointly drive a depletion in coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\), while the opposite is true when La Niña occurs[22](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR22 "Gagan, M. K. et al. Temperature and surface-ocean water balance of the mid-Holocene tropical western Pacific. Science 279, 1014–1018 (1998)."),[23](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR23 "Stevenson, S. et al. Characterizing seawater oxygen isotopic variability in a regional ocean modeling framework: Implications for coral proxy records. Paleoceanography 30, 1573–1593 (2015)."). Moreover, coral reconstructions from other basins could also be used to reconstruct the ENSO characteristics because of inter-basin teleconnections[24](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR24 "Cai, W. et al. Pantropical Climate Interactions. Science 363, eaav4236 (2019)."),[25](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR25 "Kim, J.-W. & Yu, J.-Y. Single- and multi-year ENSO events controlled by pantropical climate interactions. npj Clim. Atmos. Sci. 5, 88 (2022)."). For example, based on coral \({\rm{Sr}}/{\rm{Ca}}\) reconstructions from South China Sea, Jiang et al.[26](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR26 "Jiang, L., Yu, K., Tao, S., Li, Y. & Wang, S. Abrupt increase in ENSO variability at 700 CE triggered by solar activity. J. Geophys. Res. Oceans 128, e2022JC019278 (2023).") found that ENSO variability during 665–749 CE was enhanced substantially relative to the 1980–2014 period, perhaps due to the solar activity. However, most current studies have focused on interannual scale variability of ENSO, with few analyses on the monthly characteristics of ENSO in the historical periods.

One potential limit of coral records is that they are sparsely distributed in space, posing a challenge to represent basin-wide ENSO characteristics comprehensively. Paleoclimate Data Assimilation (PDA) optimally combines available paleoclimate proxies with climate models[27](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR27 "Hakim, G. J. et al. The last millennium climate reanalysis project: framework and first results. J. Geophys. Res. Atmos. 121, 2016JD024751 (2016)."),[28](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR28 "Tardif, R. et al. Last millennium reanalysis with an expanded proxy database and seasonal proxy modeling. Climate 15, 1251–1273 (2019)."), providing potentially one approach to reconstruct the spatial and temporal characteristics of ENSO more comprehensively from the sparse coral records. At present, there are studies based on the PDA method to reconstruct the global climate, including ENSO seasonal and interannual variability, ENSO spatial patterns, ENSO teleconnection and its responses to volcanic eruption during the historical period[29](#ref-CR29 "Steiger, J. N., Smerdon, J. E., Cook, E. R. & Cook, B. I. A reconstruction of global hydroclimate and dynamical variables over the Common Era. Sci. Data 5, 180086 (2018)."),[30](#ref-CR30 "Sanchez, S. C., Hakim, G. J. & Saenger, C. P. Climate Model Teleconnection Patterns Govern the Niño-3.4 Response to Early Nineteenth-Century Volcanism in Coral-Based Data Assimilation Reconstructions. J. Clim. 34, 1863–1880 (2021)."),[31](#ref-CR31 "Zhu, F. et al. A re-appraisal of the ENSO response to volcanism with paleoclimate data assimilation. Nat. Commun. 13, 747 (2022)."),[32](#ref-CR32 "Luo, X. et al. Last Millennium ENSO Diversity and North American Teleconnections: New Insights From Paleoclimate Data Assimilation. Paleoceanogr. Paleoclimatol 37, e2021PA004283 (2022)."),[33](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR33 "Hu, J., Dee, S., Parajuli, G. & Thirumalai, K. Tropical Pacific Modulation of the Asian Summer Monsoon Over the Last Millennium in Paleoclimate Data Assimilation Reconstructions. J. Geophys. Res. Atmos. 128, e2023JD039207 (2023)."). However, there are few PDA studies on monthly ENSO characteristics. It therefore remains unclear if the sparse coral data can be used to reconstruct monthly SST variability of sufficient accuracy. As a preparation for using PDA to reconstructing monthly ENSO variability over the last millennium, here, we first evaluate the method of reconstructing tropical monthly-resolution SST variability over the instrumental period 1880–2000 by applying a new paleoclimate data assimilation method[34](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR34 "Sun, H., Lei, L., Liu, Z., Ning, L. & Tan, Z. An analog offline EnKF for paleoclimate data assimilation. J. Adv. Model. Earth Syst. 14, e2021MS002674 (2022).") to the new coral data set: CoralHydro2k[35](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR35 "Walter, R. M. et al. The CoralHydro2k Database: a global, actively curated compilation of coral δ18O and Sr/Ca proxy records of tropical ocean hydrology and temperature for the Common Era. Earth Syst. Sci. Data 15, 2081–2116 (2023)."). We conducted verification and analysis from multiple aspects, including seasonal and monthly SST anomalies, seasonal cycle and phase-locking characteristics, and the temporal evolution and spatial distribution characteristics of ENSO events. Our study suggests the feasibility of PDA on coral data for monthly SST variability, providing the foundation for the PDA reconstruction of monthly SST variability of the last millennial in our next study.

## Results

### Improvement on monthly SSTA through data assimilation

Within the new CoralHydro2k data set[35](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR35 "Walter, R. M. et al. The CoralHydro2k Database: a global, actively curated compilation of coral δ18O and Sr/Ca proxy records of tropical ocean hydrology and temperature for the Common Era. Earth Syst. Sci. Data 15, 2081–2116 (2023)."), we used 204 spliced corals in the tropical ocean (Fig. [1](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig1)). In particular, three spliced corals are located within the Niño3.4 region (5°S–5°N, 170°–120°W; black rectangle in Fig. [1](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig1)) over the Palmyra Island (ref. [36](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR36 "Dee, S. G. et al. No consistent ENSO response to volcanic forcing over the last millennium. Science 367, 1477–1481 (2020)."); hereinafter, D20), Christmas Island (ref. [37](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR37 "Cobb, K. M. et al. Highly Variable El Niño–Southern Oscillation Throughout the Holocene. Science 339, 67–70 (2013)."); hereinafter, CM1), and Fanning Island (ref. [38](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR38 "Sanchez, S. C. et al. A Continuous Record of Central Tropical Pacific Climate Since the Mid-nineteenth Century Reconstructed From Fanning and Palmyra Island Corals: A Case Study in Coral Data Reanalysis. Paleoceanogr. Paleoclimatol 35, e2020PA003848 (2020)."); hereinafter, FN1), where the hydrological conditions are strongly dominated by ENSO activities. These three corals have high resolution and a long-time range in the modern period, so they are used as the main cases for analysis. The climate model simulations to be used are the CESM-LME simulations[39](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR39 "Otto-Bliesner, B. L. et al. Climate variability and change since 850 CE: An ensemble approach with the Community Earth System Model. Bull. Am. Meteor. Soc. 97, 735–754 (2016)."), from which off-line PDA are used and the assimilated SST field in the tropical Pacific is compared against the observation of ERSSTv5[40](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR40 "Huang, B. et al. Extended Reconstructed Sea Surface Temperature Version 5 (ERSSTv5): Upgrades, validations, and intercomparisons. J. Clim. 30, 8179–8205 (2017).") for the instrumental period 1880–2000 CE.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41612-024-00816-w/MediaObjects/41612_2024_816_Fig1_HTML.png)

The colored circles represent the location of the corals. The color scale represents the correlation coefficients of the coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) time series with the Niño3.4 index. The black rectangle denotes the Niño3.4 region.

In general, PDA starts with prior estimate of the field to be analyzed, and updates the prior field by comparing the difference between the observed proxy and an estimate of the proxy based on the prior (i.e. the innovation). The weight of innovation is determined by the Kalman gain matrix, which is mainly composed of the covariance matrix of the prior data and the error of the proxy data. For AOEnKF[34](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR34 "Sun, H., Lei, L., Liu, Z., Ning, L. & Tan, Z. An analog offline EnKF for paleoclimate data assimilation. J. Adv. Model. Earth Syst. 14, e2021MS002674 (2022)."), the prior fields consist of a 100-member ensemble of monthly mean values, that chosen based on the criterion of “similarity” between sample states and observational proxies. Notably, to ensure enough samples for the prior fields and to avoid influences from different backgrounds, the prior fields are chosen from the entire CESM-LME archive of all forcing simulations for the period 850–2005 CE. A mapping from the climate state variables to the observed proxy record through a proxy system model (PSM) (e.g. a linear regression model between local SST and coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\)). Poorly fitted coral proxies have large residual variance and get relatively less weight.

We first compare the relationship between the observed local SST anomalies (SSTA) and mean raw coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies with that between the observed and assimilated local SSTA for both winter (DJF) and annual mean. The correlations between coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) and the observed local SSTA at the three Niño3.4 sites are ~0.7, statistically highly significant (*p* < 0.01) (Fig. [2a–c](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig2)). The correlations of the assimilated local SSTA and observed local SSTA are improved to ~0.8 (*p* < 0.01) over all the three sites, suggesting the positive effect of the dynamic constraint in the data assimilation (Fig. [2d–f](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig2)).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41612-024-00816-w/MediaObjects/41612_2024_816_Fig2_HTML.png)

Scatter plot of the DJF/ANN mean coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies (x-axis; unit: ‰) and the DJF/ANN mean observed SSTA (y-axis; unit: °C) from (**a**) D20; (**b**) CM1; (**c**) FN1 sites; (**d**–**f**) same as (**a**–**c**) but for assimilated SSTA (x-axis; unit: °C) and observed SSTA (y-axis; unit: °C).

We further compare the DJF/ANN mean of observed Niño3.4 index, coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies and assimilated Niño3.4 index in the representation of coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) for the El Niño and La Niña events and the corresponding improvements from the data assimilation. We found that the correlations between coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) and Niño3.4 index at three sites are also relatively high, ranging 0.54–0.79 (*p* < 0.01) (Fig. [3a–c](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig3)), while the D20 \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies at high and low tails deviate from the Niño3.4 index \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) (Fig. [3a](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig3)), indicating that the magnitudes of strong El Niño/La Niña events are underestimated or overestimated in the coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstructions. Then, the DJF/ANN mean assimilated Niño3.4 index and mean observed Niño3.4 index are compared (Fig. [3d](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig3)). It is found that the correlations between the assimilated Niño3.4 index and the observations were improved at ~0.73 (*p* < 0.01), especially with high/low values closer to the observation, indicating improved representations on extreme ENSO events through data assimilation.

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41612-024-00816-w/MediaObjects/41612_2024_816_Fig3_HTML.png)

Scatter plot of the DJF/ANN mean coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies (x-axis; unit: ‰) and the DJF/ANN mean observed Niño3.4 (y-axis; unit: °C) from (**a**) D20; (**b**) CM1; (**c**) FN1 sites; (**d**) scatter plot of the DJF/ANN mean assimilated Niño3.4 (x-axis; unit: °C) and the DJF/ANN mean observed Niño3.4 (y-axis; unit: °C).

This improvement of the assimilated SST over raw coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) is also robust for the variability of monthly data, and other seasons (Fig. [4](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig4), Supplementary Figs. [1](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#MOESM1)–[8](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#MOESM1)). In Fig. [4a](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig4), it can be seen that the correlations between assimilated local SSTA and observed local SSTA are greater than that of coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies and observed local SSTA in both monthly data and other seasons. For Niño3.4 index (Fig. [4b](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig4)), the correlations between the monthly/ANN mean assimilated Niño3.4 index and observed Niño3.4 index are similar to that of coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies and observed Niño3.4 index. The correlations between the JJA/SON/DJF mean assimilated Niño3.4 index and observed Niño3.4 index are much greater than that of coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies and observed Niño3.4 index. One exception is the correlation between the spring (MAM) mean assimilated Niño3.4 index and the observed Niño3.4 index, which is lower than that of coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies and the observed Niño3.4 index. This discrepancy may be attributed to the spring predictability barrier[41](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#ref-CR41 "Fang, X., Zheng, F., Liu, Z. & Zhu, J. Decadal modulation of ENSO spring persistence barrier by thermal damping processes in the observation. Geophys. Res. Lett. 46, 6892–6899 (2019).").

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41612-024-00816-w/MediaObjects/41612_2024_816_Fig4_HTML.png)

**a** The correlation coefficients (y-axis) between the observed local SSTA, coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies and assimilated local SSTA in different seasons (x-axis). The triangle-marked curve represents the correlation between observed local SSTA and raw coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies, the square-marked curve represents the correlation between observed local SSTA and assimilated local SSTA. The cyan, blue and pink curves represent D20, CM1 and FN1 corals, respectively; **b** The correlation coefficients (y-axis) between observed Niño3.4 index, coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies and assimilated Niño3.4 index in different seasons (x-axis). The triangle-marked curve represents the correlation between observed Niño3.4 index and raw coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) anomalies, the square-marked curve represents the correlation between observed Niño3.4 index and assimilated Niño3.4 index. The cyan, blue, pink and yellow curves represent D20, CM1, FN1 corals and assimilated Niño3.4 index, respectively.

### The seasonal cycle and ENSO phase-locking

The seasonal cycle and phase-locking between D20 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstruction and the observed local SST are first compared. It can be found that the seasonal cycle of D20 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) (Fig. [5b](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)) is close to that of observed local SST (Fig. [5a](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)) with the largest depletion and enrichment in August and February, respectively, indicating that D20 coral is a good representation to the seasonal cycle of local SST. The assimilated SST seasonal cycle is also consistent with the observation (Fig. [5c](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)). The phase-locking comparisons show that the peak months of the largest variances in coral \({\delta }^{18}O\) and assimilated SST at D20 site tend to be in boreal autumn (SON) (Fig. [5](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)b, c), which has a time-lead compared to the observation (NDJ) (Fig. [5a](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)), but the bias of the assimilated results is smaller. Therefore, the seasonal cycle of the observed coral \({\delta }^{18}O\) at D20 site can be reproduced well in assimilated SST, although there is a small mismatch in phase-locking.

![figure 5](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41612-024-00816-w/MediaObjects/41612_2024_816_Fig5_HTML.png)

Seasonal cycle and ENSO phase-locking from **a** observed ERSSTv5 local SST (unit: °C), **b** D20 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) (unit: ‰), and **c** assimilated local SST (unit: °C). **d**–**f** are same as **a**–**c** but for CM1 site; **g**–**i** same as **a**–**c** but for FN1 site. In **a**–**i**, the thin gray lines denote the seasonal cycle of single years, and the thick blue lines denote the mean seasonal cycle, with the blue shading and the blue bars both representing the standard deviation of SST (unit: °C) or \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) (unit: ‰).

Next, we compare the seasonal cycle and phase-locking at CM1 site. In observations, the local SST shows its highest value in June and the lowest value in February (Fig. [5d](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)), whereas those of coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) respectively peak in June and April (Fig. [5e](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)). The coldest month of the assimilated SST occurs in October, which is earlier compared to the observed local SST (Fig. [5f](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)), and this bias originates from the CESM-LME model (Supplementary Figure 9b). These findings imply the inability to reproduce the observed seasonal cycle features of either \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstruction or model simulation alone. The peak month of observed local SST is in December (Fig. [5d](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)), whereas the peak months of \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) are in November and April (Fig. [5e](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)), which differ substantially from the observation. Interestingly, the assimilated local SST showed peak months in November and December (Fig. [5f](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)), consistent with the observation (Fig. [5d](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)). This indicates that data assimilation is capable of reducing some seasonal bias of the raw coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) records by integrating the information from both coral and model.

The FN1 site is close to the CM1 site, so the observed highest and lowest SST at FN1 site are in July and February, respectively. The seasonal cycle in FN1 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstruction is relatively flat, with the largest enrichment and depletion occurring in August and February, respectively (Fig. [5h](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)). The seasonal cycle of assimilated local SST is similar to observations and coral proxy, with the highest and lowest SST in July and January, respectively (Fig. [5i](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)). The peak month of observed local SST appears in NDJ with the largest standard deviation (Fig. [5g](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)). The peak months of FN1 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) show two preferred peaks in November and May (Fig. [5h](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)). The assimilated local SST showed peak months in OND (Fig. [5i](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig5)), the resemblance of observed SST from assimilated local SST compared with raw coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) at FN1 site also demonstrates the improvement through data assimilation.

The observed SST at the three sites all show the highest temperature in summer to autumn and the lowest temperature in spring, and the peak months of observed SST at three sites all appear in winter (DJF). However, there are small time-lead in the phase-locking of D20 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\), and biases in the seasonal cycle and phase-locking of CM1 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) in the April, and the phase-locking of FN1 coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) has two small peaks in winter and spring. The inconsistent phase-locking features in the three coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstructions indicate the inability of coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) to directly represent ENSO phase-locking feature. We also examined seasonal cycle and phase-locking for a wider tropical Pacific coral proxy network in Supplementary Note 1.

Alternatively, as a crude indicator of phase-locking, we calculated the phase-locking strength of observations, coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstructions, and data assimilation as the ratio of the difference between winter and summer standard deviations to the sum of winter and summer standard deviations (Fig. [6](/articles/s41612-024-00816-w#Fig6)). The results show that the phase-locking strength of assimilation is weaker than observed phase-locking, but stronger than that in coral \({\delta }^{18}{\rm{O}}\) reconstruction. Furthermore, we also investigated the seasonal cycle and phase-locking features of Sr/Ca

enriched_points.expanded_content: Coral skeletal records, particularly δ±8O values, provide high-resolution reconstructions of historical sea surface temperatures, effectively extending our understanding beyond the instrumental data era.
Coral growth patterns mirror environmental conditions, such as temperature and salinity, enabling insights into past climate events and their impacts on marine ecosystems and coral health.
Coral proxies reveal long-term climate trends and variability, including El Niño-Southern Oscillation characteristics, enhancing our understanding of historical climate dynamics over millennia.
